findings.purpose: GPU-accelerated zero-shot sentiment analysis pipeline for Korean skincare product reviews, using a multilingual NLI transformer to produce per-document overall sentiment, aspect-level sentiment (moisture, effectiveness, texture, absorption), and satisfaction scores. Runs on RunPod (remote GPU) against preprocessed parquet files stored in AWS S3.

keyLibs: konlpy==0.6.0 (KoNLPy/Okt — upstream preprocessing, not sentiment inference)

findings.architecture: Two-script pipeline. `preprocess_from_s3.py` runs locally: loads raw parquet files from S3 for a given date, runs Korean morpheme extraction (KoNLPy/Okt) via multiprocessing, and writes `preprocessed_reviews.parquet` back to S3 at `data/reviews/raw/{date}/preprocessed_data_{date}/`. `main_gpu.py` runs on RunPod: loads that preprocessed parquet, instantiates `AsyncSkincareReviewAnalyzer` (in `utils/sentiment_analysis_gpu.py`), and processes all rows through an async batched NLI inference loop. The core class loads `MoritzLaurer/mDeBERTa-v3-base-xnli-multilingual-nli-2mil7` from HuggingFace, converts it to fp16 on CUDA, and classifies each review text against Korean-language hypothesis strings using zero-shot NLI (entailment probability as score). Three hypothesis sets are evaluated per review: overall sentiment (positive / negative / neutral), satisfaction level (very / somewhat / unsatisfied), and four skincare aspects (moisture, effectiveness, texture, absorption) each with three polarity hypotheses. Results are written as a wide Parquet file to S3 at `data/reviews/raw/{date}/processed_results_{date}/sentiment_results.parquet` with a `_manifest.json` sidecar. A downstream script (`upload_to_db.py`) reads the merged results from S3 and writes them into a versioned PostgreSQL table.

keyCommands.purpose: Download raw parquet for a date from S3, run KoNLPy keyword extraction, upload preprocessed_reviews.parquet back to S3.
Same as above with explicit multiprocessing worker count.
Load preprocessed parquet from S3, run mDeBERTa-v3 zero-shot inference on GPU, upload sentiment_results.parquet + manifest to S3.
Same as above with --resume flag (flag is parsed but checkpoint logic is not yet implemented).
Run with explicit throughput tuning parameters (defaults shown).

notableDecisions.decision: Zero-shot NLI via mDeBERTa-v3 instead of a fine-tuned sentiment head
notableDecisions.rationale: Enables classification against arbitrary Korean hypothesis strings without labeled training data; the multilingual model handles Korean natively. Entailment probability is used directly as the score for each label.

notableDecisions.decision: fp16 (half-precision) inference on GPU via model.half()
notableDecisions.rationale: Reduces GPU memory footprint and increases throughput on Ampere hardware; combined with TF32 matmul and cuDNN benchmark flags for maximum CUDA utilization.

notableDecisions.decision: Entailment index resolved dynamically from model config id2label
notableDecisions.rationale: Avoids hardcoding the NLI label order; model introspection ensures the correct probability column is extracted regardless of label ordering. A prior hardcoded version (`probs[:, 2]`) was replaced by this approach.

notableDecisions.decision: Async batched inference with asyncio.Semaphore concurrency cap
notableDecisions.rationale: Allows large datasets to be queued as concurrent batch tasks while bounding maximum in-flight GPU work via semaphore (default 200); outer chunking (50k rows) keeps memory bounded.

notableDecisions.decision: Keyword columns from preprocessing are forwarded into sentiment output unchanged
notableDecisions.rationale: Downstream PostgreSQL table expects a wide schema combining both keyword and sentiment signals; the sentiment script preserves upstream keyword columns by copying them into results_df.

findings.currentStatus: dormant — last meaningful commit 2026-02-11 (mDeBERTa-v3-base model switch)
findings.dataFlow: 1. Raw reviews arrive as Parquet files in S3 at `s3://trendmix/data/reviews/raw/{date}/`. 2. `preprocess_from_s3.py` downloads them, runs KoNLPy/Okt morpheme extraction (local multiprocessing), and uploads `preprocessed_reviews.parquet` back to S3 at `data/reviews/raw/{date}/preprocessed_data_{date}/`. 3. `main_gpu.py` runs on RunPod: loads that Parquet, chunks it (default 50k rows/chunk), feeds each chunk through `AsyncSkincareReviewAnalyzer.process_chunk()` which batches rows (64/batch) and sends them through `batch_inference()` (inference batch 512). Each review is evaluated against 3 sets of Korean hypothesis strings; the NLI entailment probability is the score. 4. Output is a wide Parquet written to S3 at `data/reviews/raw/{date}/processed_results_{date}/sentiment_results.parquet` plus a `_manifest.json`. 5. `upload_to_db.py` (downstream, out of scope) reads merged S3 data and writes into a versioned PostgreSQL table (`product_review_NNN`) with all sentiment and keyword columns as FLOATs/TEXTs.

contentPointers.claim: Three output dimensions per review: overall sentiment (positive/negative/neutral), satisfaction level (very/somewhat/unsatisfied), and four skincare aspects (moisture/effectiveness/texture/absorption) each with positive/negative/neutral scores
Output column schema: SA_positive, SA_negative, SA_neutral, aspect_{aspect}_{polarity} (12 columns), satisfaction_very, satisfaction_somewhat, satisfaction_un — all FLOAT rounded to 3 decimal places
Input source: preprocessed Parquet from S3 at s3://trendmix/data/reviews/raw/{date}/preprocessed_data_{date}/preprocessed_reviews.parquet
Output destination: Parquet written to S3 at s3://trendmix/data/reviews/raw/{date}/processed_results_{date}/sentiment_results.parquet plus _manifest.json sidecar
Batch inference: inference_batch_size=512 (default), batch_size=64, chunk_size=50000; async concurrency capped at max_concurrent=200 via asyncio.Semaphore
GPU optimizations: fp16 via model.half(), TF32 matmul, cuDNN benchmark, AMP autocast, CUDA cache flush between inference batches

Preprocessing (upstream, separate script): KoNLPy/Okt morpheme extraction producing keywords_nouns, keywords_adjectives, keywords_verbs, keywords_adverbs, keywords_{category} columns appended to Parquet
--resume flag is parsed but checkpoint logic is not implemented; the flag has no effect
contentPointers.evidence: main_gpu.py:118 (argparse stores but is never consumed in main())

suggestedSections: Model identity — provider, model ID, language, NLI framing
Input source — S3 path convention, Parquet schema, review_text column
Preprocessing step — separate script, KoNLPy/Okt, keyword column passthrough
Inference flow — async batching, chunk/batch/inference-batch sizing, GPU setup
Output schema — 20 sentiment/satisfaction/aspect columns, FLOAT rounded to 3dp
Output destination — S3 path, Parquet + manifest JSON
CLI commands — main_gpu.py and preprocess_from_s3.py invocations
Known issues — --resume unimplemented, no GPU fallback for fp16 dtype mismatch on CPU

gapsFromBrief: No caching or rate-limit handling around model API calls: the model runs locally on GPU (no external API), so there is no API rate limiting or caching layer. The brief asked about this; the answer is it does not apply.

--resume flag: parsed as argparse argument (main_gpu.py:118) but never consumed in the main() function — no checkpoint/resume logic exists. Content-agent should flag this as a known issue rather than a capability.

S3 bucket name 'trendmix' is hardcoded as default but overridable via --bucket; no .env.sample or equivalent config file documents required env vars (AWS credentials assumed from environment/instance role on RunPod).
recommendations: The --resume flag should either be implemented or removed; its presence implies a capability that does not exist and could mislead operators running large batches.
An .env.sample documenting required AWS env vars (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_DEFAULT_REGION) would improve reproducibility — currently undocumented.

The ingredient-embeddings agent-doc exists at src/content/agent-docs/ingredient-embeddings.mdx and is a valid related entry — both are NLP subsystems in the same pipeline family and both use HuggingFace models.
